summary: I was born in Louisville, Kentucky, which is where my story begins before I was raised in Eastern Kentucky. Louisville is the place where my roots start, but my upbringing took me to the hills and small towns beyond. It’s always been a part of my identity, even as I moved away and pursued music and life elsewhere.

I was raised in Eastern Kentucky, specifically in Floyd County, which shaped much of my childhood and early memories. Growing up there, I was surrounded by family, music, and the traditions of small-town life. The sense of community and togetherness from that region deeply influenced who I am today.

Floyd County, Kentucky, is where I spent my early years, especially around Floyd Park in Letcher County. It’s a place filled with family memories and the music that I grew up with. That area really grounded me in the culture and traditions of Appalachian Kentucky.

Letcher County, Kentucky, was part of my upbringing, particularly around Floyd Park. It’s where a lot of my family gatherings and musical experiences took place. The county’s small-town atmosphere and close-knit community were a big part of my childhood environment.

My grandfather, Wilbur Shorty Jamerson, played football for Bear Bryant at the University of Kentucky. He was nicknamed 'Shorty' because he was five-nine and 160 pounds. He met my grandmother at UK, and that connection to the university and sports is a proud part of my family history.

Bear Bryant was the legendary coach my grandfather, Wilbur Shorty Jamerson, played football for at the University of Kentucky. Hearing about my grandfather’s time under Bear Bryant connects me to a significant era in Kentucky sports history. It’s a proud legacy that ties my family to that period and place.

The University of Kentucky is where my grandfather, grandmother, and aunt Mary Osborne all went. It’s where my grandfather played football under Bear Bryant and where my family roots in education and sports run deep. I grew up around stories and memorabilia from that era, which makes UK a central part of my family’s story.

My family’s roots go back to the pre-World War I era, a time when my great-grandmother and others essentially crawled out of the mud to build their lives. That era represents a strong foundation for who I am, reflecting a time before modern capitalism when life was about gathering and community. I feel deeply connected to those ancestors and their resilience.

Although I’m not really part of the Nashville ecosystem, I’ve been involved in the Kentucky music scene for the last twenty years. Nashville represents the mainstream music industry that I’m somewhat outside of, as I focus more on creating something different and authentic in Kentucky. It’s a place I’m aware of but not deeply embedded in.

I was recently seen performing at The Burl, where I felt a strong connection with the crowd and was able to work the audience authentically. The Burl is a place where I can share the music and togetherness that have always been important to me. Playing there reminded me of the energy and presence I’ve sought since childhood.
